coredata.dc:title: Tailoring phases of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C nanocomposites using syringyl and guaiacyl-rich biomass-derived carbon nanodots for electrochemical application

coredata.dc:description: Biomass-derived carbon nanodots (CNDs) hold promise as effective reducing agents for metal oxide nanoparticles yet understanding the intricate interplay with CND structure remains challenging. This study explores the impact of lignin types, specifically syringyl (S), and guaiacyl (G) units in CNDs on metal oxide phases and their electrochemical activity toward dopamine oxidation. We design phases of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C nanocomposites, using hazelnut carbon nanodots (HS-CNDs (S-rich)) and beetroot carbon nanodots (BS-CNDs (G-rich)) via a one-pot hydrothermal technique. Our findings show S units in HS-CNDs promote α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS, while G units in BS-CNDs favor α (β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS. In contrast to α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS, α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS exhibits superior electrochemical performance in dopamine oxidation due to its larger electrochemical active surface area, higher absorbance capacity, and shortened electron transfer length. Moreover, α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS nanocomposites demonstrate remarkable dopamine selectivity, achieving rapid detection response in 10 s with a low LOD of 4 nM within a broad linear range (0.05–0.3 μM), demonstrating impressive reproducibility (97.5 %), stability (96.4 %), and works in real-time human urine detection with a recovery rate of ranging from 94.57 % and 102.2 %. Therefore, the utilization of biomass-derived CNDs, particularly S and G units-rich CNDs, in tailoring the phases of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C nanocomposites for electrochemical dopamine detection is promising.

head.ce:title: Tailoring phases of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C nanocomposites using syringyl and guaiacyl-rich biomass-derived carbon nanodots for electrochemical application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass-derived carbon nanodots (CNDs) hold promise as effective reducing agents for metal oxide nanoparticles yet understanding the intricate interplay with CND structure remains challenging. This study explores the impact of lignin types, specifically syringyl (S), and guaiacyl (G) units in CNDs on metal oxide phases and their electrochemical activity toward dopamine oxidation. We design phases of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C nanocomposites, using hazelnut carbon nanodots (HS-CNDs (S-rich)) and beetroot carbon nanodots (BS-CNDs (G-rich)) via a one-pot hydrothermal technique. Our findings show S units in HS-CNDs promote α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS, while G units in BS-CNDs favor α (β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS. In contrast to α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS, α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS exhibits superior electrochemical performance in dopamine oxidation due to its larger electrochemical active surface area, higher absorbance capacity, and shortened electron transfer length. Moreover, α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS nanocomposites demonstrate remarkable dopamine selectivity, achieving rapid detection response in 10 s with a low LOD of 4 nM within a broad linear range (0.05–0.3 μM), demonstrating impressive reproducibility (97.5 %), stability (96.4 %), and works in real-time human urine detection with a recovery rate of ranging from 94.57 % and 102.2 %. Therefore, the utilization of biomass-derived CNDs, particularly S and G units-rich CNDs, in tailoring the phases of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C nanocomposites for electrochemical dopamine detection is promising.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The realm of carbon nanodots (CNDs) has attracted considerable interest due to their versatile attributes, such as tunable photoluminescence, high quantum yield, low toxicity, smaller size, and biocompatibility. These properties make them promising in various applications, and their synthesis methods, including top-down or bottom-up approaches, are actively explored for sustainable and eco-friendly production [1–4]. A green synthesis route involves a hydrothermal process using non-toxic precursors, ensuring a safer method to obtain CNDs, [5–11]. Various carbon sources including carbon fiber [12], graphene [13], citric acid [14], ascorbic acid, vitamins, glucose [15], biomass [16–20], etc., can be employed for CND preparations. Among these sources, the conversion of whole lignocellulosic biomass stands out as a green strategy for obtaining CNDs from inexpensive natural resources in a more sustainable and eco-friendly manner [21]. Lignin, a three-dimensional network-branched phenolic polymer found in terrestrial plants, is a component of lignocellulose [22,23] and decomposes during whole biomass valorization, serving as a source of aromatic conjugated structure [24,25]. CNDs possess diverse properties, and notably, they exhibit dual capabilities, functioning as both electron acceptors and donors concurrently. Consequently, they hold the potential for catalyzing the reduction of metal salts to their metallic forms, serving as reducing agents [26].

Numerous studies highlight the use of biomass-derived CNDs as green reducing, stabilizing, and structural directing agents for the synthesis of metal oxide nanoparticles, eliminating the need for toxic chemicals and expensive heavy metals under harsh reaction conditions [27,28]. For instance, Mandani et al. demonstrated the creation of an Au@C-dot nanocomposite under aqueous conditions, where CNDs act as robust stabilizers by creating a fine coating around Au nanoparticles (Au NPs), akin to self-assembled monolayers [29]. Similarly, reduced CNDs have the ability to directly reduce chloroauric acid, yielding gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) without the necessity of additional reducers or stabilizers and the hydroxyl groups (-OH) on the surface of CNDs serve dual role as both reducers and stabilizers in the synthesis of AuNPs which exhibit inherent catalytic activity toward reduction of 4-nitrophenol with sodium borohydride [30]. Moreover, CNDs exhibit impressive catalytic activity for a range of organic reactions due to their innate acidic and oxidative characteristics. Their surface, featuring carboxylic functionality, serves as an efficient stabilizing agent for oxide nanoparticles and the cooperative interactions between CNDs and metal oxides could further enhance the catalytic performance of these nanoparticles [31]. Another study presented a straightforward method for crafting Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) with precise shapes and sizes using NE/EA CDs, streamlining the fabrication process and reducing material and energy expenses [26].

Additionally, CNDs employed to create a uniform and finely dispersed Fe2O3-graphene composite aerogel in a single-step conversion process from a precursor mixture using laser -assisted conversion of carbon nanodots facilitates the even distribution of 6.5 nm Fe2O3 nanoparticle while forming a highly conductive carbon matrix [32]. Another study also mention, the integration of CNDs onto the TiO2 surface was explored for the synthesis of TiO2 nanorods decorated with CNDs, enhancing photoelectrochemical water oxidation [33]. According to the reference [31] interactions between CNDs and iron oxide nanoparticles exhibited efficient electron transfer, resulting in a novel nano-catalyst with significant catalytic efficiency and reusability, particularly in synthesizing quinazolinones. Furthermore, the self-assembly of the 3D carbon dots/Ni(OH)2 hierarchical structures was driven by electrostatic force between the carbon dots and cations, serving as a novel type of structure-directing agent, the carbon dots played dual role in shaping the morphology of the resulting products and enhancing the performance of the supercapacitor [34]. Similarly, lignin also acts as a reducing agent for the synthesis of metal oxide [22]. For example, in the eco-friendly synthesis of nanoparticles (NPs) like Au, Ag, Pd, AuAg bimetallic, Au-Ag-Pd trimetallic, and Fe3O4-Au-Ag-Pd nanocomposites, a method was devised to produce these particles in both a liquid solution and at the interface between solid and liquid phases [35]. However, both lignin and CND hardly employed as a phase directing agent for metal hydroxide and oxide. For instance, Amino acid-mediated carbon dots are used as phase-directing agents for synthesis α-Ni(OH)2 where the CND source is derived from β-Alanine and ethylene glycol [36]. However, the CND source involves toxic chemicals and harsh synthesis conditions. To the best of our knowledge, no study is reported on phase directing properties of lignin in CND. Herein, we synthesis non-functionalized syringyl (S)-rich and guaiacyl (G)-rich CND as-phase directing agents for metal oxides.

In this study, we investigated the influence of lignin type especially syringyl (S) and guaiacyl (G) unit in CNDs, on the phases of metal oxide and electrocatalytic activity, particularly with ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C and its ability to oxidize dopamine. We compared hazelnut shell carbon nanodots (HS-CNDs) and beetroot skin carbon nanodots (BS-CNDs), primarily composed of S and G units, respectively, to prove our hypothesis. Through deliberate design, we created ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C nanocomposites, demonstrating its transformation based on the presence of the S and G units. The synthesis involves chelating Fe2+ with biomass-derived CNDs. Our study revealed that S-rich CNDs promote the formation of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS nanocomposites, whereas G-rich CNDs favor the formation of α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS. Unlike α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS, α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS nanocomposites exhibit superior electrochemical activity for the oxidation of dopamine. This superiority is attributed to the larger electrochemical active surface area, higher absorbance capacity, and short electron transfer length of the α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS nanocomposites compared to α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS nanocomposites. Furthermore, α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS nanocomposite displayed exceptional anti-interference properties against nine biomolecules commonly found in the human body, along with high reproducibility and stability in dopamine detection. Real-time analysis using human urine validated its practical applicability as a modified sensor in diverse environments.

Hazelnut shells (HS) and beetroot skins (BS) were obtained by delicately removing the outer layers of raw nuts and vegetables, followed by thorough washing with deionized water (D.I.) cleanliness assurance. Subsequently, the meticulously cleaned shells and skins were left to air-dry at room temperature until achieving a constant weight. They were then carefully ground into a fine powder and sifted through a No. 8 standard mesh, resulting in samples donated as: HS, and BS, respectively.

Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), uric acid (UA, >99.0 %), glucosamine (GA, 99 %) and epinephrine hydrochloride (Ep) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (South Korea). Glutathione (Glu, 95 %), catechol (Ca, +99 %), ascorbic acid (AA, 99.0 %), dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (98.0 %), and potassium dihydrogen phosphate (99.0 %) were sourced from Acros Organics (South Korea). Additionally, urea, and caffeine (CAF, 99 %) were obtained from Alfa Aesar (South Korea). Arginine hydrochloride (AH, >99 %) was purchased from Kanto Chemical (Japan) and Crown Guaranteed Reagents (Japan), respectively. All chemicals were used as received without further treatment. The deionized water (DI) employed had an electrical resistance of 18.2 MΩ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of HS, and BS carbon nanodots
ce:section.ce:para: A known mass of hazelnut shell (HS) powder was mixed with 100 mL of deionized water and subjected to sonication for over 5 min for good dispersion. The resulting solution was then transferred into a sealed 150 mL Teflon-Lined autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 12 h in a hydrothermal oven. After natural cooling at room temperature, the brownish liquid, identified as carbon nanodots (CNDs) was separated from the solid residue (hydro char) via centrifugation. This process led to the creation of HS-CNDs, as depicted in Scheme 1 . A similar procedure was followed to produce BS-CNDs.

A one-pot hydrothermal method was employed for the synthesis of ferrihydrite /α-Fe2O3@C nanocomposites. In this process, 0.01 g of FeCl2·4H2O, 0.5 g of Urea, and 0.2 g of NH4F were added to 20 mL of HS-CNDs and subjected to sonication for over 5 min. The introduction of Fe2+ to the CND solution caused a noticeable color change from light brown to black shown in Fig. S1, indicating the static quenching of the fluorescence emitted by the CNDs. The resulting mixture was then transferred into an 80 mL Teflon-Lined autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 12 h. After natural cooling at room temperature, the solid residue was separated from the liquid using a centrifuge and subsequently washed several times with deionized water and ethanol until its pH reached approximately 7.0 (neutral). The obtained sample was then dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 8 h. The resulting black powder was designated as α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS. The as-prepared sample exhibited a distinct morphology, as shown in FE-SEM images, Fig. 4, depicting an interconnected nanoparticle-like structure, as outlined in Scheme 1. A similar procedure was employed to produce α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS, nanocomposite using BS-CND. Additionally, a control sample of β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 was prepared using the same approach except CNDs incorporation.

3 mg of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS was added to 200 μL of dimethylformamide (DMF), 10 μL nafion (5 % solution) as a binder and then sonicated for 60 min to form black ink. 4 μL of the ink was drop-cast on a well-polished glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with an area of 0.071 cm2 and then dried at 50 °C for 15 min. The resulting electrode is denoted as α-FeOOH/-Fe2O3@CHS/GCE. A similar procedure was used to prepare all other electrodes and commercial α-Fe2O3 as a control sample.

The redox modulating property of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS toward DA oxidation was investigated based on (i) limit of detection (LOD), (ii) interference effect, (iii) sensitivity, (iv) stability, and (v) reproducibility, and (vi) response time. Cyclic voltammogram (CV) and Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV) were the two major electroanalytical techniques employed for these investigations. The addition method was employed to construct the calibration curve. The procedure was as follows: typically, a known volume of DA stock solution was added to an electrochemical cell containing PBS at pH = 7.0 and stirred for 3 min at room temperature. Afterward, the appropriate electroanalytical technique was applied. All the experiments were repeated at least three times (n ≥ 3) and their average values were used.

The morphology of the as-prepared materials was characterized using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, S-2400, Hitachi) equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) operated at 15 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis (XPS, VG Scientific ESCALAB 250) was performed using monochromatic Al-Ka radiation at 1486.6 eV. Crystal structure analysis was done with X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis on a D8 ADVANCE equipped with a diffractometer using a Cu Ka radiation source operated at 40 kV and 40 mA. FTIR spectra were collected on NICOLRT iS5 (Thermoscientic), while electrochemical measurements were performed on SPELEC (Korea) and ZIVE MP1 (Korea) electrochemical workstations with the modified GCE as working electrode, Pt. as counter electrode and Ag/AgCl (in 1 M KCl) as the reference electrode. Electrochemical Impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in potential static mode at a given bias potential. A frequency range of 100 mL to 100 kHz under AC perturbation of 10 mV was used. All the measurements were done at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of as- prepared Carbon nanodots (CNDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In our investigation, the pH measurements of as-prepared CNDs indicated that HS-CNDs had a pH of 3.3, whereas BS-CNDs showed a pH of 4.7, as detailed in Table S1. These pH values suggest an acidic nature of CNDs, consistent with previous findings [37]. The acidity can be attributed to the transformation of lignin products into diverse soluble polymeric compounds during polymerization and condensation processes [13,38]. Lignin, as noted in reference [39], contains various inter-molecular linkages, with the β–O–4 linkage being one of the most prevalent, constituting 40–60 % of all linkages. Hydrolysis of these bonds in wood is a significant implication of lignin degradation [40].Thus, the observed variations in pH values strongly suggest the presence of lignin in the as-prepared CNDs. This assertion is further supported by the visual appearance of freeze-dried HS-CNDs (black) and BS-CNDs (dark brown), as depicted in the photographic image in Fig. 1C. The black color of HS-CNDs suggests a higher content of condensed aromatic structures [41]. Furthermore, Fig. 2D illustrates the absorbance spectra of the as-prepared HS-CNDs and BS-CNDs, showing absorption in the UV–vis range beginning at the same wavelength (250 nm–300 nm) attributed to π -π transitions corresponding to the CC bond [42–44] with different peak intensities. Notably, the peak intensity for HS-CNDs is measured at 2.02, while for BS-CNDs, it stands at 3.24. Moreover, the absorbance spectra revealed that BS-CNDs exhibited a normal distribution, whereas HS-CNDs displayed an average distribution. This suggests that HS-CNDs can absorb a wider range of wavelengths, while BS-CNDs have a more specific wavelength absorption profile. These findings substantiate our hypothesis that CNDs derived from different biomass sources exhibit distinct absorption characteristics.

In Fig. 1(A-B), the TGA/DTG thermograph of the as-prepared CNDs displays weight loss within the temperature range of 30-150 °C, attributed to moisture loss in the CNDs [45]. Another weight loss, occurring gradually between 200 and 400 °C, is linked to the decomposition of hemicellulose and cellulose components [46]. The most substantial weight loss is observed beyond 400 °C, primarily due to the decomposition of lignin within the CNDs. This underscores that the composition of the as-prepared CNDs is predominantly composed of lignin compared to other lignocellulose constituents (such as cellulose and hemicellulose). Consequently, it inferred that aromatic fragments originating from the biomass's lignin possibly condensed to form clusters within the CNDs. The proportion of lignin in the as-prepared CNDs varies based on its source. According to the TGA/DTG analysis, the estimated percentage distributions were 35.6 % and 45.5 % for BS-CNDS and HS-CNDs, respectively. This indicates the presence of higher lignin content in HS-CNDs compared to BS-CNDs. Additionally, the maximum degradation peaks for HS-CNDs and BS-CNDs were observed at 486 and 589 °C, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 1(A-B). This divergence in peak temperatures suggests distinct thermal stabilities among the CNDs, implying variations in the composition and structural aspects of lignin present in each biomass-derived carbon nanodot.

TEM image reveals the carbon nanodots are spherical as shown in Fig. 1(D - E) which is comparable to literature [47]. Moreover, the HR-TEM image shown in Fig. 1(D - E, insets) revealed that the presence layers with inter-tuned structure in the CND, suggesting the presence of different lignin units. The XRD spectrum of as-prepared CNDs exhibited two weak broad peaks located at 2θ value of 16o assigned to disordered carbon and 26o attributed to the inter-alkyl chain distance of densely packed carbon [48] as shown in Fig. 1F. The weak broad peaks suggest the poor crystalline nature of CNDs. The XPS analysis demonstrates the presence of C, O, and N on the surface as-prepared CNDs. In both HS-CND and BS-CND, the C1s XPS spectrum revealed four peaks at binding energies of 283.3, 284, 286.4, and 287.6 eV assigned to CC, CC, CO, and C=O/C=N respectively [42]shown in Fig. 2(A - B). Additionally, the O1s spectrum exhibited peaks at 529.7 and 531.7 eV indicating the presence of CO and C-OH /C-O moieties [49], respectively, shown in Fig. 2(C-D). Interestingly, the intensity contribution of C-OH /C-O in HS-CND is higher compared to BS-CND suggesting the presence of higher surface oxidizing moieties in HS-CND. Analysis of the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2(E - F)). displayed a peak at 398.3, 399, and 400.6 eV suggesting the existence of pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogen in both CNDs, respectively [50–52]. However, the percentage area of pyrrolic N in BS-CND is higher compared to HS-CND (Table S4), suggesting a higher concentration or a more favorable environment for pyrrolic N in BS-CND. The result from XPS indicates distinct chemical compositions and surface functionalities of HS-CND and BS-CND. This finding was also confirmed with TGA and UV–vis analysis.

Additionally, Fig. 3C shows FTIR spectra of both as-prepared CNDs exhibited similar peak stretches at wavenumbers 3203, 2932, 1800–1600, 1407, 669 corresponding to the presence of -OH/N-H, -C–H stretching, CO stretching of carbonyl group [17], C-O-C, and CH at varied intensities, respectively [2]. Moreover, the S unit exhibited stronger band intensities in HS-CNDs (1331 cm−1, 1120 cm−1, and 805 cm−1), compared to BS-CNDs which have very weak S bands at the assigned aromatic skeletal vibrations [53,54]. Furthermore, the intense peak for the G unit of lignin presented in BS-CNDs at band 1032 cm−1 corresponds to CC stretching. This suggests that HS-CNDs contains more S unit lignin whereas the BS-CNDs has more G unit lignin [55]. This observation agrees well with the TGA, XPS, and UV–vis analysis.

We further studied the proton nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H NMR) on the as-prepared CNDs to distinguish the composition of CNDs as shown in Fig. 3(A-B). The 1H NMR spectra of HS-CNDs and BS-CNDs show the protons signal between 6.0 ppm and 8.0 ppm attributed to aromatic protons [40]. Thus, the proton signal at 6.4–to 6.1 ppm is attributed to the S unit [56] and the proton signal from 7.25 to 6.8 ppm corresponds to the G unit [57]. It is seen from Fig. 3(A-B) that G unit proton signal is more prominent over the S unit for BS-CNDs than HS-CNDs which is the S unit dominant over the G unit. This indicates that HS-CNDs are composed of a relatively larger amount of S unit lignin than BS-CNDs which are composed of a relatively large amount of G unit. The 1H NMR spectra results further confirms the FTIR, XPS, and TGA analysis.

Fig. 4B shows FE-SEM image of α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS shows nanoparticles at low S unit and high G unit in CNDs. Fig. 4A α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS nanocomposites show interconnected nanoparticles for high content of S unit in CNDs. It is observed from the SEM image as the S unit increases in CNDs the nanoparticle transforms to an interconnected nanoparticle structure. Whereas the control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 without CNDs took a long nanorod-like structure as shown in Fig. 4C. Furthermore, the TEM images of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS and α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS reveals a nanosheet structure grown on CND nanosphere, as depicted in Fig. 4(A-2) and (B-2) respectively. However, the nanosheet in α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS tends to aggregate more compared to α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS. HRTEM image also show long nanosheet for α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS compared to α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS (Fig. 4(A-3) and (B-3)). On the other hand, the TEM and HR-TEM of β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 demonstrate a nanorod-like structure (Fig. 4(C-2) - (C-3)). These observations suggest that CND can influence the morphology of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3. This could be attributed to Ostwald ripening which is the growth mechanism by dissociation and recrystallization of the smaller particle on the more stable surface of the larger nanoparticles [58]. Still, the potential cause of observation of Ostwald ripping in this study is the effect of S and G unit lignin in CNDs, which acts as nucleation sites for the growth of the former and limits the long nanorod formation. FE-SEM image suggests (Fig. 4) the interaction between CNDs and Fe2+ nanocluster depends strongly on the structure of S and G unit in CNDs. Therefore, ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C morphology could be well controlled by how Fe2+ interacting types of lignin in CNDs. EDX spectrum and elemental mapping analysis confirmed that the presence of iron (Fe), oxygen (O), and carbon (C) was distributed uniformly in the as-prepared α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS.

Fig. 6A shows the TGA/DTG analysis of as-prepared samples investigated in the range of 30-800 °C under air. The first weight loss exhibited in the range of 30-100 °C attributed to the moisture absorbed on the surface of as-prepared samples. The second weight loss demonstrated in the range of 120–300 °C, attributed to the decomposition of FeOOH to α-Fe2O3, affirming its presence in the sample [59]. The percentages of FeOOH in α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS, α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS and control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 were 7.8 %, 5.4 % and 2.9 %, respectively. This suggests a higher presence of FeOOH in α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS compared to α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS and the control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3, likely linked to the higher S unit content in the CNDs. Notably, the control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 exhibited the lowest FeOOH content, implying that incorporating Fe2+ on the surface of the S unit in CND enhances FeOOH formation. This indicates that water adsorbed on the catalyst surface can augment catalytic rates. The third weight loss exhibited beyond 300 °C indicating the breakdown of carbon within the CND, revealing the presence of CND in ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C. Therefore, the mass percentage of ferrihydrite /α-Fe2O3 in the nanocomposite is determined from the residue remaining after heating to 800 °C, where FeOOH transforms to α-Fe2O3 under air [60]. In α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS, α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS and the control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3, were 68.9 %, 76.2 %, and 90.05 %, respectively. Despite using an equal mass of Fe2+ precursor for both CNDs, the lowest α-Fe2O3 content was found in α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS nanocomposite. This could be attributed to Ostwald ripening in the hydrothermal reactor, where Fe is absorbed onto the surface of the S unit in CNDs, acting as a nucleation site during dissociation and recrystallization on the CND surface. Fe3+ in the solution that did not absorb (or interact) onto the S unit is lost during washing. The absorbed Fe3+ on the S unit provides better α-Fe2O3 distribution in α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS. Whereas the G unit has a higher affinity toward Fe3+, favoring the aggregation and excess α-Fe2O3 in α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS nanocomposite. This was evidenced by using parsley leaf carbon nanodots as a control, which are rich in dominant G and low S units, indicated a higher absorption of Fe3+ compared to HS-CNDs. This suggests that as the S unit decreases or G increases in the composition, it promotes more Fe3+ formation. This emphasizes the dependence of the interaction between CNDs and Fe2+ nanoclusters on the type and quantity of lignin in the CNDs. Overall, the TGA analysis indicates the significant influence of S and G units in CNDs on ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C nanocomposites.

The X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS was analyzed, the fitting revealed peaks corresponding to akaganite (β-FeOOH) (JCPD#13–0157), α-Fe2O3 hematite (JCPD #1–1053), and a limited amount of goethite (α-FeOOH) (JCPD# 29–0713) with a rhombohedral structure. In addition to the peaks assigned to CND, these findings indicate the presence β-FeOOH, α-FeOOH and α-Fe2O3 (Fig. 5B). The increase in S units in CNDs led to the transformation of the β-FeOOH phase into α-FeOOH through Ostwald ripening in α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS [61]. Interestingly, pumpkin skin CNDs, possessing a similar pH value to BS-CNDs, facilitated the formation of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@C. This implies that the phase transformation from β-FeOOH to α-FeOOH is not solely dependent on the pH value of the CNDs but is influenced by the type of lignin in CNDs, such as S and G units. Hence α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS was fitted to the plane of goethite α-FeOOH (JCPD#29–0713) and α-Fe2O3 hematite (JCPD#1–1053) with rhombohedral structure, indicating its composition of α-FeOOH, α-Fe2O3 and Carbon [62,63]. Furthermore, the sharp peaks observed in the XRD patterns of α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS and β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 suggest an excess presence of α-Fe2O3, which is confirmed by TGA analysis. These results highlight the ability to direct Fe2+ into a specific phase by adjusting the S and G units in CND source. Specifically, S-rich CNDs promote the formation of the α-FeOOH phase with a highly exposed {110} facet, while G-rich CNDs favor the β-FeOOH phase with a highly exposed {310} facet. In summary, XRD analysis suggests that the S and G units in CND play a crucial role in influencing the crystalline facets of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C nanocomposites.

The FTIR spectrum for α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS and α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS nanocomposites is depicted in Fig. 6B. Both as-prepared nanocomposites exhibited analogous peak stretching at 535 and 578 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibration of FeO relative to α-Fe2O3 [64]. Additionally, a band at 795 cm−1 was identified as the -OH stretching vibration of α-FeOOH, [65]. Notably, an extra band at 847 cm−1 was observed for α(β)-FeOOH/Fe2O3@CBS and the control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3, corresponding to the -OH stretching vibration of β-FeOOH according to reference [66]. The FTIR analysis indicates the presence of α-FeOOH in both synthesized nanocomposites, while the presence of β-FeOOH in α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS and the control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 is confirmed with XRD spectra. This implies that the S-rich CNDs promote the formation of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS and the higher G-rich CNDs favor the formation of α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS. Therefore, the FTIR suggests that the S and G units in CNDs influence the phases of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C nanocomposites. Additionally, the Raman spectrum of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS exhibited peaks at 244, and 552 cm−1, corresponding to stretching of the metal and hydroxide, confirming successful α-FeOOH formation [59,67], as shown in Fig. S2. The characteristic peaks appear at 404 and 610 cm−1 assigned to α-Fe2O3 [68]. On the other hand, α(β)-FeOOH/Fe2O3@CBS exhibited additional peaks at 535 and 710 cm−1 confirming the presence of β-FeOOH [69], revealing the potential influence of CND type on the phase of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3. Interestingly, the presence of CND was indicated by prominent peaks at 1350 and 1565 cm−1 corresponding to C in both samples [4], which confirms the successful formation of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C. Therefore, the Raman spectrum also supports the result obtained from FTIR and XRD analysis.

Fig. 6C shows the band gap of the as-prepared material which was determined from the Tauc relation using Equation (αhv)0.5 = A(hv-Eg). Where a E
g is photon energy, h is Planck's constant (6.6 × 10−34 J.s), v is the frequency of the electromagnetic radiation. c is the speed of light (3.0 × 108 ms−1), and λ is wavelength in meter. The result clearly showed that the band gap of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@C depended intensely on the contents of S units and increased from 2.5 e V for α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS for G units which is 2.99 eV for α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS. This suggests that S units promote fast electron transfer in α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS nanocomposite [70].

The N1s spectra were deconvoluted into three bands at ∼398.1, 399.8, 400.7, and 402.1 eV. attributed to the presence of pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, graphitic-N, and oxidized-N, respectively [50–52] (Fig. 8(E - F)). α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS exhibited the highest percentage of graphitic-N 52.9 % which is higher than α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS (19.2 %) (Table S5.) indicating that the S-rich in CND boosts the graphitization nanocomposite. Therefore, α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS nanocomposite is highly graphitic compared to α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS and control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 nanocomposite which will be favorable for fast electron transfer [76].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic voltammograms (CV) behavior of DA at modified GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The redox activity of DA on the modified GCE was investigated using CV experiments conducted in the presence and absence of 13.03 μM DA/PBS solution (Fig. 9 ). Whereas no peak has been observed on these electrodes in the absence of dopamine. However, upon the addition of 13.03 μM DA, a pair of well-defined redox peaks were observed due to the electrocatalytic oxidation of DA at the modified electrode. However, the observed oxidation peak current (I pa) and oxidation peak potential (E pa) strongly depend on the nature of the modified electrode. Prominently, α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS modified electrode exhibited a higher oxidation peak current of 2.994 μA which is 2.4×, 5.92×, and 6.92× higher than that of α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS (1.238 μA), control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 (0.505 μA), and comm. α-Fe2O3 (0.433 μA) of modified GCE, respectively (Fig. 9A). From the comparison of CV analysis, this work demonstrates the strong dopamine oxidation peak current for α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS modified GCE electrode whereas the pristine β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 exhibited weak peak current. This is a clear indication that α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS nanocomposite-modified GCE electrode demonstrates superior electrocatalytic activity toward dopamine oxidation. This is ascribed to the fact that α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS modified electrode, due to their advantages of the facet of α-FeOOH exposed could possess more active sites for reaction to occur.

Additionally, the onset potential (Von-set) of DA oxidation on α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS modified electrode is 0.12 V which is lower than that α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS (0.126 V), control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 (0.126 V) and comm. α-Fe2O3 (0.166 V) (Fig. 9B), reveals the α-FeOOH phase nanocomposite is a more electrochemical active material [77]. A sudden increase in current intensity could be observed after the onset potential for the α- FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS/GCE is 1101.6 %, 1.53×, 4.7× and 5.83× higher than those of α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS, control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3, and comm.α-Fe2O3 of modified GCE, respectively, (Table S4). This signifies that α-FeOOH phase nanocomposites are a much better DA redox mediator than β-FeOOH nanocomposites. Additionally, the CV peak current of the control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 modified electrode is lower than that of modified by α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS nanocomposite modified electrodes. It is clearly shown that the electrochemical activity of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS electrocatalyst is dependent on the α-FeOOH phase. Moreover, the investigation of α-Fe2O3 loading toward the electrochemical oxidation of dopamine is shown in Table S7. Notably, the α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS exhibited lower mass loading (2.067 mg) compared to α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS (2.286 mg). However, the α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS demonstrate a significantly higher activity compared to α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS. Specifically, α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS exhibited 2.994 μA which is approximately 2.41× higher than α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS (1.238 μA), despite containing more α-Fe2O3. This observation suggests that the superior electrochemical activity of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS cannot be attributed to the loading of α-Fe2O3. Instead, it indicates that the enhanced electrochemical activity of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS may originated from the phases of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C. Furthermore, the electrochemical activity of pristine HS- CNDs and BS-CNDs toward oxidation of 26.06 μM DA is shown in Fig. S3. HS-CNDs and BS-CNDs exhibited oxidation peak current and oxidation peak potential of (0.86 μA, 0.262 V) and (0.867 μA, 0.39 V) respectively, which is lower compared to the control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 (0.92 μA, 0.23 V). However, when these CNDs synergized with Fe2+, the oxidation current is enhanced dynamically as seen in Fig. 9A. Nevertheless, the enhancement in oxidation peak current is contingent upon the formation of ferrihydrite/ α-Fe2O3@C which relies on how Fe2+ interacted with the S and G units in CNDs.

The effect of the exposed phase of FeOOH helps to generate Fe2+/Fe3+ whereas C from CND facilitates electron transfer kinetics and absorbance because the Fe2+/Fe3+ redox couple is a familiar electroactive site for DA detection [78–88]. The ability to generate Fe2+/Fe3+ redox couples is usually associated with better electron transfer kinetics in Fe3O4 [89] electron transfer length (ẟ) from electroactive Fe sites to C and vice versa. In the α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS a single hydroxide phase in which α-FeOOH is present as its was confirmed from XRD, XPS and FTIR. From TGA, α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS nanocomposite owns a more prominent α-FeOOH phase and evenly distributed α-Fe2O3 thus the ẟ from α-FeOOH to α-Fe2O3 and then to C this contributing to short electron transfer length which facilitates electron transfer Fe2+/Fe3+. On the other hand, in the α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS nanocomposite, the dominance of β-FeOOH phase along with low α-FeOOH phase and excessive α-Fe2O3 on the C surface thus the ẟ from α-FeOOH to β-FeOOH and to α-Fe2O3 and to reach carbon C which further increase the electron transfer length compared to α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS from electroactive Fe sites to C and vice versa. A schematic illustration of how δ affects effective electron transfer is shown in Scheme 2 . Furthermore, the other factors that affect the DA oxidation are investigated and presented in the sections below.

The adsorption capacity of the as-prepared sample was determined from Equation Γ = Q/nFA [90] where n is the number of transferred electrons (taken as 2), A is the geometric electrode surface area, and F is Faraday constant, Q is the total charge. The total charge (Q) is determined from the area under CV for as-prepared material as shown in Fig. S3. Hence, the adsorption capacity of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS modified electrode is 6.57 × 10−6 mol/cm2 which is 3.03×, 4.2× and 5.56× distinctly higher than those modified by α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS (2.18×10−6 mol/cm2), control β-FeOOH/Fe2O3 (1.54×10−6 mol/cm2), and comm. α- Fe2O3 (1.183 × 10−6 mol/cm2), respectively shown in (Fig. 9C), This is in accord with and follows the trend for DA current response. Indicating that the α-FeOOH phase nanocomposite has a higher adsorption capacity which enhances the redox activity toward DA oxidation. Thus, the adsorption capacity can be a strong candidate for major rate-determining factors for DA oxidation.

Furthermore, we have checked the effect of ECSA, which is determined according to Randles-Sevcik Eq. (1) for reversible from the square root of scan rate vs peak current shown in Fig. 10D.

where Ipa is the current response of DA, n is the number of electrons transferred in the process of DA, A is the electrode surface area, C is the analytical concentration of DA in 0.05 M PBS, D is the diffusion coefficient which has a value of 6.5 × 10−6 cm2 and v is the scan rate (V/s) at 50mv/s. Thus, the ECSA of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS is 8.22 × 10−4 cm−2 which is 2.23×, 4.39× and 5.024× noticeably higher than those α(β)-FeOOH/Fe2O3@CBS, control β-FeOOH/Fe2O3, and comm. α- Fe2O3 as shown in Fig. 9D, respectively. Interestingly the ratio of oxidation peak current, and ECSA of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS compared to another modified as-prepared electrode is proportional. For instance, the ratio of oxidation peak current and ECSA of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS to α (β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS is 2.41× and 2.23×, respectively. Similar trends were observed on other modified as-prepared electrodes as well (Table S6) Consequently, the enhancement of the electrochemical active area could offer more reactive active sites for adsorption and subsequent oxidation of dopamine, which eventually promotes the sensing performance toward biomolecular. Generally, the ECSA for α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS, α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS, is quite higher compared to comm. α-Fe2O3 and control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 suggesting that α-FeOOH phase nanocomposite enhances the electrochemical active surface area [80,91–94].

Additionally, the effect of the conductivity of the modified electrode on the electrochemical activity toward DA oxidation was investigated. Thus, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the as-prepared electrode was conducted under the frequency range of 100 mHz to 100 KHz in the presence of 13.03 μM DA in 0.05 M PBS as shown in Fig. 9E. α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS/GCE exhibited small semi-circle compared to α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS /GCE, and control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3. Indicating that the α-FeOOH phase nanocomposite promotes a noticeable acceleration of redox kinetics of DA happened on the surface of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS. This suggests that a fast electron transfer is on the surface of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS nanocomposite modified electrode than α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS /GCE nanocomposite modified electrode. This indicates that the conductivity might be a factor for DA oxidation on the surface of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS/GCE.

The effect of scan rate on DA redox peak of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS /GCE was studied using CV in the presence 13.03 μM DA in 0.05 M PBS by varying the scan rates from10 to 180 mV/s as shown in Fig. 10. It is seen that both the oxidation (I
pa ) and reduction (I
pc ) peak current increased linearly as a function of the square root of scan rates with a linear regression correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9996, I
pa = 0.47v
0.5
–0.276 and R2 = 0.995, I
pc = −0.491v + 1.525, respectively. Suggests that diffusion-controlled process on the surface of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS modified electrode [92,93,95]. Additionally, the ratio of oxidation peak current to reduction peak current (Ipa/Ipc) of 1.5, a value >1.0, suggesting a quasi-reversible diffusion-controlled redox process according to the reference [92]. A similar redox mechanism was also noticed for α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS and β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 modified electrode diffusion-controlled process as shown in Fig. S4-S6.

Moreover, the effect of scan rate on oxidation peak potential was also investigated as shown in Fig. 10A. Accordingly, as the scan rate increases the peak potentials gradually change. Moreover, the oxidation peak potential shifts to the positive direction whereas the corresponding reduction peak potential shifts to the negative direction which causes an increase in peak-to-peak separation in the redox peak position. This suggests that the charge transfer kinetics partially restrict the rate of redox reaction [80]. Consequently, − the change in peak-to-peak separation of potential with scan rate and the electron transfer(k
s ) rate can be determined from the Laviron Eqs. (2)–(4) [96].

P is the peak separation of the DA redox couple, and n is the number of electrons transferred. Therefore, n and k
s values are determined to be 2.1–2 and 0.655 s−1 respectively. This highlights two-proton and two electrons were transferred at the surface of the modified electrode during the DA redox process which agrees with the literature [97]. Ks value reveals that the faster electron transfer through α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS modified electrode is 0.6555 s−1, 1.6×, 1.751×, and 1.83× higher than α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS, control β-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3 and comm.α-Fe2O3 respectively (Table S7). Thus, k
s could be a rate-determining factor for efficient DA redox mediator on the surface of the modified electrode [98,99].

Moreover, the enhancement mechanism of CND on the electrochemical activity of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C nanocomposite involves several key factors: (1) tuning the phase of ferrihydrite/Fe2O3 confirmed from the XRD analysis, this phase tuning likely affects the electrochemical properties and performance of nanocomposite. (2) Enhancing electrochemical conductivities of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3, confirmed from the UV–vis band gap analysis, this improvement in conductivities is crucial for efficient electron transfer during electrochemical processes supported by EIS [36]. (3) The functional groups present in the CNDs surface such as hydroxide and carbonyl groups are confirmed from FTIR and XPS. These functional groups could provide more active sites and improve wettability, thus promoting the adsorption of electrolyte ions in the nanocomposite [100–102]. Moreover, they offer negative charge to the nanocomposites which attract the positively charged DA hence it improves the selectivity of the nanocomposite.

The interference response of electrochemical analysis from CV response on α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS /GCE sensing toward 13.03 μM DA was evaluated in 0.05 M PBS at pH of 7.0 in the presence of more than nine (9) potential substance which is presented in human body such as ascorbic acid, uric acid, glucose, epinephrine, glutathione, diclofenac, catechol, arginine, and caffeine. Fig. S7(A-I) shows the oxidation peak current of individual DA resulted in almost no change in the presence of these electroactive biomolecules. This strongly suggests the anti-interference capability of the modified electrode in the case of concurrent determination of DA in a complex system containing all the above-mentioned biomolecules which are presented in our body in higher concentration than DA. This excellent performance of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS /GCE could be ascribed to the presence of hydroxy, carbonyl and S units of the surface of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS/GCE sensor which gives negative charge to repel other negatively charged biomolecules and attract the positively charged DA. This ascertains that the proposed sensor has high selectivity toward DA detection in the presence of interfering analytes [101,103].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive detection of DA and response time
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 11 (A-B) shows the CV response of α-FeOOH/Fe2O3@CHS /GCE and α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS electrode with increasing DA concentrations from 0.0529 μM to 4.22 μM in 0.05 M PBS (pH of 7.0.). These results reveal that α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS electrode exhibits increasing current response with increasing DA concentration in the range of 0.0529 μM to 0.3 μM and had a good linear response with correlation coefficient R2 = 0.998, I
pa = 1.505C
DA + 0.476. From the linear calibration curve, the sensitivity and LOD of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS/GCE modified electrode are determined to be 1.525 μA.μM−1 and 4 nM, respectively. Revealing that α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS tends to possess excellent sensitivity and nanomolar level detection [104] where LOD was determined from 3σ/S (where σ: standard deviation of 10 background current and; S: sensitivities of the sensor) [105]. This low LOD value is much lower than the LOD reported in the literature [106,107]. Moreover, this result is also analogous to those of other reported modified electrodes indicating that the proposed α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS /GCE has the potential capability for the electrochemical sensing of DA shown in Table 1 . Furthermore, the sensitivity of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS modified electrode has quite remarkable difference which is 2.52× more sensitive compared to α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS as shown in Fig. 11(A-B), respectively. Amperometry current-time technique (i-t) was used for detecting the concentration of DA in 0.05M PBS (the potential: 0.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl) as shown in Fig. S8A. A well-defined, stable, and steeply increased amperometry current response was obtained after addition of 13.03 μM DA solution within less than 10s response time. Suggesting that very fast response time for DA oxidation.

The reproducibility of modified α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS was also carried in CV current response toward 13.03 μM DA investigated at four different electrodes as shown in Fig. S8B. All four electrodes have exhibited the same anodic peak current difference with a high percentage recovery of 97.5 % indicating the acceptable reproducibility of the proposed sensor. The stability of the modified α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS /GCE carried in CV current response to ward 13.03 μM DA was investigated with five days intervals for a month after storing the modified electrode at -4 °C shown in Fig. S8C. 96.4 % of the initial response of anodic peak current was obtained indicating that the satisfactory storage stability of proposed sensor.

Real-time analysis of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS /GCE DA sensor was carried out on human urine. The human urine stock was centrifuged to remove the impurities. After that known concentration of DA (13.03 μM) in collected human urine was detected in 0.05 M PBS (pH 7.0) on CV as shown in Table 2 . The α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS/GCE detects DA in real samples with a recovery of 94.57 %–102.2 %. Note that in addition to DA, many different biomolecules are present in human urine. Consequently, from the result, those biomolecules show no prominent interference during DA detection of DA in real sample analysis. Indicating that α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS/GCE is applicable in direct determination of DA in real sample analysis with excellent selectivity.

We successfully induced static quenching interaction between Fe2+ and biomass- derived carbon nanodots (CNDs), especially syringyl (S) and guaiacyl (G) units-rich CNDs to design phases of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C via a one-pot hydrothermal method. Various physicochemical analyses, including TGA, TEM, HR-TEM, Raman, 1HNMR, UV–vis, XRD, FTIR, FE-SEM, and XPS, were employed to characterize the as-prepared material. The composition and structure of inherent lignin in the biomass played a crucial role in determining the phase of FeOOH/Fe2O3 during the formation of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C. The synergistic effect between S units in hazelnut carbon nanodots (HS-CNDs) and Fe2+ led to the formation of α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS. On the other hand, the presence of G units in beetroot carbon nanodots (BS-CNDs) promoted the formation of α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS. Remarkably, ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C phases were achieved using biomass carbon nanodots rich in S and G unit lignin without the requirement for harsh conditions. In comparison to α(β)-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CBS, α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS exhibited enhanced electrochemical activity toward dopamine (DA) oxidation due to its larger electrochemical active surface area, higher absorbance capacity, and shorter electron transfer length. Additionally, the nanocomposite demonstrated outstanding anti-interference properties against nine (9) common biomolecules found in the human body, coupled with high reproducibility and stability in dopamine detection. Moreover, the proposed sensor, α-FeOOH/α-Fe2O3@CHS, exhibited superior electrocatalytic performance compared to state-of-the-art iron-based electrode materials. Therefore, the utilization of biomass-derived carbon nanodots, especially those rich in S and G units lignin, shows great potential in deigning diverse phases of ferrihydrite/α-Fe2O3@C nanocomposites for electrochemical applications, particularly in dopamine detection.

Meseret Ethiopia Guye: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Richard Appiah-Ntiamoah: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mintesinot Dessalegn Dabaro: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shimelis Kebede Kassahun: Investigation. Hern Kim: Supervision, Funding acquisition.
